subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution: The properties of elements change with a change in atomic number.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I: The standard reduction potential $E^{\circ}_{\text{Al}^{3+}/\text{Al}}$ is $-1.66$ V, which is highly negative, indicating that aluminium has a strong tendency to lose electrons and is highly electropositive. The standard reduction potential $E^{\circ}_{\text{Tl}^{3+}/\text{Tl}}$ is $+1.26$ V, which is positive, indicating that thallium is less electropositive than aluminium. Thus, Statement I is true.

Statement II: Boron has a very small atomic size, resulting in a very high sum of its first three ionization enthalpies. This high energy requirement prevents boron from losing three electrons to form $\text{B}^{3+}$ ions, so it forms only covalent compounds. In contrast, aluminium has a larger size and a significantly lower sum of its first three ionization enthalpies, allowing it to easily form $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ions in its compounds. Thus, Statement II is true.

Both Statement I and Statement II are true.

Answer: Both Statement I and Statement II are true

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution: On moving Left to Right along a period. Atomic Radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math> decreases. Electronegativity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math>Increases. Electron gainenthalpy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math> Increases. Ionisation Enthalpy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo></math> Increases.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution: Catenation is the tendency to link with one another through covalent bonds to form chain and rings, i.e., catenation. This is because bonds are very strong in the carbon family. On moving down the group, the size increases and the tendency to show catenation decreases because bond enthalpy decreases. The order of catenation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>></mo><mi>Si</mi><mo>></mo><mi>Ge</mi><mo>></mo><mi>Pb</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Pb</mi></math> does not show catenation.

chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution: Ionisation energy decreases with increase in the size of atom. Na, K, Rb belongs to alkali metal and down the group ionization energy decreases as size of atom increases down the group.  Among the given elements, Sc being small has the highest ionization energy (Na= 496, K=419; Sc =631 kJ mol-1)

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution: In a period from left to right atomic size decreases.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
Analyzing Statement I: The order given is Al > Mg > Mg²⁺ > Al³⁺. However, the correct atomic radii order should be Mg > Al because Mg is in group 2 (period 3) while Al is in group 13 (period 3), and moving right across a period decreases radius. The correct order would be Mg > Al > Mg²⁺ > Al³⁺. Therefore, Statement I is FALSE.

Analyzing Statement II: Electron gain enthalpy magnitudes follow: Cl (highest, ~349 kJ/mol) > Br (~325) > S (~200) > O (~141). This is because Cl has the highest tendency to gain an electron due to its position and electronic configuration. Statement II is TRUE.

Since Statement I is false and Statement II is true, the answer is option (3).

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
On moving down in a group atomic radii increases due to successive addition of extra shell hence
$$
\mathrm{O} < \mathrm{S} < \text { Se }
$$
Further As is in group 15 having one less electron in its $p$ orbital hence have higher atomic radii than group 16 elements.
i.e., $\mathrm{O} < \mathrm{S} < \mathrm{Se} < \mathrm{As}$

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
First ionization enthalpy generally increases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge and decreasing atomic radius. However, exceptions occur based on electron configuration stability.
For 3rd period elements, the approximate ionization enthalpy values are: Al (578), Si (787), P (1012), S (1000), Cl (1251) kJ/mol.
(1) Al has lower IE than Si because removing 3p¹ is easier than removing one electron from 3p².
(2) S has slightly lower IE than P despite being to the right because P has a half-filled p-orbital (3p³) which is more stable.
(3) Cl has the highest IE as it approaches a filled p-orbital.
The correct order is: Al < Si < S < P < Cl

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution: As difference in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>rd</mi></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> ionisation energies is high so atom contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> valence electrons.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: azimuthal quantum number of valence shell
electron –electron interaction in the outer orbitals
Principal quantum number of valence shell
solution:
For isoelectronic species the size is compared by nuclear charge.
Size of isoelectronic species is compared on the basis of their nuclear charge.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
In a period, as we move from left to right, the atomic size decreases and the effective nuclear charge increases.

Due to the large atomic size and low effective nuclear charge, the element at the extreme left (alkali metal) has the lowest first ionisation enthalpy in the period.

As we move to the extreme right (excluding noble gases), the elements (halogens) have the smallest atomic size and highest effective nuclear charge in the period. They require only one electron to complete their octet. Thus, they have the highest negative electron gain enthalpy.

Therefore, the first ionisation enthalpy of the extreme left element is the lowest, and the negative electron gain enthalpy of the extreme right element is the highest.

Answer: lowest and highest

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
The expected order of melting points of group 13 elements is B>Al>Ga>In>Tl.

But due to structural changes, melting point instead of decreasing, increases from Ga to Tl and Ga has the lowest melting point. Hence, the actual order is B>Al>Tl>In>Ga

Melting point

Al→933 K

Ga→303 K

In→430 K

Se→490 K

Ga has low meting point and it is closer to metalloid in periodic table.

Negative, but less negative than the first
A more negative value than the first

Second electron gain enthalpy is always positive for every element because to overcome repulsion between an anion and electron,  energy has to be supplied.

O − + e − → O −2  ;  Δ H eg =+ve

The element having high value of Electron gain enthalpy (magnitude) will form a compound having higher ionic character so order of ionic character
$E B>E A>E C>E D$

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: None of the factors because their size is the same
Electron-electron interaction in the outer orbitals
solution:
In F-,Ne,Na+all have 1 s2,2 s2,2p6configuration. They have different size due to the difference in nuclear charge.

For isoelectronic species, the more nuclear charges, the less will be the size of the species. The valence electrons will experience a greater attractive force due to the increase in nuclear charge.

Hence, the size of isoelectronic species - F-, Ne and Na+ is affected by nuclear charge Z.

Thus, the ionic size of Na+ is the lowest, and the ionic size of F- ion is the highest.

Hence, the answer is D.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
Given $n=2$, the electronic configurations correspond to elements of the second period.

A. $ns^2 \Rightarrow 2s^2$ is Beryllium (Be).
B. $ns^2np^1 \Rightarrow 2s^2 2p^1$ is Boron (B).
C. $ns^2np^3 \Rightarrow 2s^2 2p^3$ is Nitrogen (N).
D. $ns^2np^6 \Rightarrow 2s^2 2p^6$ is Neon (Ne).

The first ionization energy generally increases across a period from left to right. However, Beryllium has a higher ionization energy than Boron due to the stable fully-filled $2s$ subshell. Nitrogen has a high ionization energy due to its stable half-filled $2p$ subshell. Neon, being a noble gas, has the highest ionization energy.

The order of first ionization energy is B $<$ Be $<$ N $<$ Ne.

Arranging the given values in increasing order: $800 < 899 < 1402 < 2080$ kJ mol$^{-1}$.

Thus, the matching is:
A. Be $\rightarrow 899$ (II)
B. B $\rightarrow 800$ (III)
C. N $\rightarrow 1402$ (IV)
D. Ne $\rightarrow 2080$ (I)

This corresponds to A-II, B-III, C-IV, D-I.

Answer: A-II, B-III, C-IV, D-I

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
The electronegativity of Ge on the Pauling scale is $1.8$. In Group 13, the electronegativity values are B ($2.0$), Al ($1.5$), Ga ($1.6$), In ($1.7$), and Tl ($1.8$). Thus, the element 'E' is Thallium (Tl).

Due to the inert pair effect, the $+1$ oxidation state is much more stable than the $+3$ oxidation state for the heavier elements of Group 13, especially Thallium. Therefore, $Tl^+$ is more stable than $Tl^{3+}$. This makes statement C incorrect.

Since $Tl^{3+}$ is relatively unstable, it readily accepts two electrons to form the more stable $Tl^+$ ion ($Tl^{3+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Tl^+$). Because it undergoes reduction easily, $Tl^{3+}$ acts as a strong oxidizing agent. This makes statement A incorrect and statement B correct.

The standard electrode potential for the reduction of $Tl^{3+}$ to Tl ($E^{\circ}_{Tl^{3+}/Tl}$) is $+1.26\text{ V}$, which is a positive value. This makes statement D correct.

Therefore, only statements B and D are correct.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
Mg have 2 electrons in valance shell and after donation of 2 electrons it converts into Mg2+ ion and attains inert gas configuration, now to ionize one more electron from inert gas configuration is very difficult, hence it is easy to ionize Mg to Mg2+.  IE2 is always greater than IE1 because on removal of e-, size decreases and effective nuclear charge increases.So Assertion is correct.

Mg2+ is a smaller ion than Mg+ and has a greater charge density. This means that Mg2+ has a stronger attraction to its electrons than Mg+ and requires more energy to remove an electron from it. So reason is also correct.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
options: For group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> elements, the stability of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math> oxidation state increases down the group.

Elements of group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> have lower ionization enthalpy values compared to those of group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> in the corresponding periods.
The group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn></math> elements are all metals.
All the elements in group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn></math> are gases.
solution: Group 16 elements have less ionization potential than Group 15 elements due to half-filled configurations of group 15 elements. So need more amount of energy to remove an electron. In the halogen family iodine is solid and bromine is liquid. In the boron family boron is non metal.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
In a period the value of ionisation potential increases from left to right with breaks where the atoms have some what stable configuration. After removing an $e^{-}$ oxygen will acquire stable configuration. Hence $\mathrm{O}^{+}$has highest second ionisation enthalpy than $\mathrm{F}^{+}$and $\mathrm{N}^{+}$.
$\therefore$ correct order is $\mathrm{O}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{N}>\mathrm{C}$
